pipeline_models._rationale: Cross-model critique. The drafter (Claude Sonnet) and the critic (GPT-5.5) are different models from different vendors. When they agree something is wrong, the signal is much stronger than one model self-critiquing. The decider (Claude Sonnet again) synthesizes the two perspectives. Same pattern gstack uses for /codex review of Claude-authored work.

series_anchor.label: Charles Comte — Traité de la propriété (Volumes I-II)
series_anchor.palette: stone grey, ink umber, weathered iron, parchment cream, single accent of muted teal
series_anchor.mood: Boundary stones, ironwork, parchment ledgers. The slow inscription of property lines onto land.
series_anchor.watercolor_treatment: Dry-brush stone textures; iron and metal rendered with single concentrated washes that pool to near-black at the deepest point and dilute to grey at the edges; teal allowed only as a small note.

work_metadata.title: Traité de la propriété: VOL I
work_metadata.author: Charles Comte
work_metadata.description: Traité de la propriété (1834) by Charles Comte presents a sweeping philosophical and legal theory of private property. Comte argues that liberty is the foundation of rights, and that occupation and appropriation of things flow naturally from human freedom. He criticizes ancient jurisprudence grounded in slavery, claiming it undermines genuine property rights. He traces the formation of property from individual acquisition of things, through communal and national levels, exploring territory, value and legal guarantees. Comte thus frames property as essential for human development and social progress.

1_context.content_excerpt:
## PREFACE

Had the work I publish today been brought to light four or five years ago, the time at which I intended for it to appear, I would have had no need, before entering upon the subject, to address my readers directly to observe that it was the continuation of another treatise I published in 1826 and 1827. Upon reading it, they would have perceived this for themselves, without needing to be told; they would have seen that I had applied the same method to the observation of phenomena of the same kind, and that I had followed the natural order of ideas, at least insofar as it was in my po

output.reasoning: The Traité de la propriété grounds property rights in the physical act of occupation and boundary-marking — the inscription of ownership onto land. A single iron surveying stake or boundary marker driven into earth evokes this perfectly: the hard edge of claimed territory, the slow legal inscription, without illustrating any narrative scene.
output.subject: A single iron boundary stake driven partway into pale earth
output.palette: weathered iron, stone grey, parchment cream with a single small note of muted teal
output.composition: The stake occupies the lower-left third of the composition, angled slightly off vertical, its top reaching no higher than mid-canvas. The upper-right two-thirds is open parchment cream negative space, breathing and unresolved. The visual weight of the iron pulls the eye downward and left.
output.watercolor_treatment: The iron stake rendered with a single concentrated wash of weathered iron pigment, pooling to near-black at the driven tip and at shadow creases, diluting outward to stone grey at the upper shaft where the pigment runs thin and cold-press paper grain shows through. A dry-brush scuff along one edge suggests oxidized surface texture. The surrounding earth is a faint raw umber wash, wet-on-wet, bleeding softly into the cream paper with no hard boundary. One small teal note appears where iron meets earth — a suggestion of verdigris, nothing more.
output.prompt: A single iron boundary stake driven partway into pale earth, painted in watercolor on warm cream cotton paper. The stake sits in the lower-left third of the composition, angled slightly off vertical, its top reaching mid-canvas at most. A concentrated wash of weathered iron pigment pools to near-black at the buried tip and along shadow creases, diluting upward to stone grey where the pigment thins and cold-press paper grain shows through the wash. Dry-brush scuffs along one edge suggest oxidized, pitted metal. A faint raw umber wash bleeds wet-on-wet around the base, dissolving into the parchment cream paper with no hard edge. One small note of muted teal marks the junction of iron and earth — a trace of verdigris, restrained. More than half the painting is open negative space.

output.reasoning: The prompt is structurally aligned with the anchor; the only material weakness is hedged positional language that could be made more exact.

The critic's single issue is a medium-severity weak language flag on 'angled slightly off vertical.' Replacing it with 'tilted five degrees off vertical' is a concrete improvement that adds precision without bloating the prompt or conflicting with the series anchor. Accept it.

decisions.reason: Replacing vague 'slightly off vertical' with a specific five-degree tilt gives the image model a cleaner positional instruction without adding length or conflicting with the anchor.

output.final_prompt: A single iron boundary stake driven partway into pale earth, painted in watercolor on warm cream cotton paper. The stake sits in the lower-left third of the composition, tilted five degrees off vertical, its top reaching mid-canvas at most. A concentrated wash of weathered iron pigment pools to near-black at the buried tip and along shadow creases, diluting upward to stone grey where the pigment thins and cold-press paper grain shows through the wash. Dry-brush scuffs along one edge suggest oxidized, pitted metal. A faint raw umber wash bleeds wet-on-wet around the base, dissolving into the parchment cream paper with no hard edge. One small note of muted teal marks the junction of iron and earth — a trace of verdigris, restrained. More than half the painting is open negative space.
output.changes_summary: Replaced 'angled slightly off vertical' with 'tilted five degrees off vertical' to sharpen the positional instruction. No other changes were made.
